trajectory.description: Observation: Empty workspace, starting ClimSim emulation task. Analysis: Need to build MLP for tabular regression - 124→128. Data is huge (~10M samples), need efficient training. Decision: Start with simple MLP baseline, train few epochs, submit eval to measure pipeline.

Observation: Loss is low (0.004) but R2 is hugely negative. Analysis: The val_y.var(axis=0) might be tiny for some dimensions — pre-normalized data should have variance ~1, but a few dims may have near-zero variance, causing huge negative R2 from tiny errors. Need to inspect. Also data is pre-normalized so var should be 1 — but evaluation uses var of y, so R2 = 1 - MSE/var. If MSE is small and var is small that's an issue. Let me check val_y stats first. Decision: investigate data and fix R2 computation; also use much larger model + more epochs since 15s/epoch is fast.

trajectory.description: Observation: 21 of 128 output dims have effectively zero variance — these are degenerate (constants). My R2 mean includes them, which destroys the metric. The eval likely uses a similar formula; if they include all dims, near-zero var dims dominate. But more likely the official eval skips them or expects predictions to match the constant. Need to: (1) compute R2 only on dims with var > threshold for monitoring, (2) for those degenerate dims, predict the mean (which in normalized space might not be 0 — val_y mean range is -0.06 to 1.76!). The data is 'pre-normalized' but means aren't all zero. Some output dims are ~constant at non-zero values. Decision: train normally, but during prediction, force degenerate output dims (low variance in train) to their training mean. Also need to look at training target stats. Train ResMLP for ~5 epochs, save train_y means/vars in checkpoint, override degenerate predictions in predict.py.

trajectory.description: Observation: Eval works! mean_r2=0.303 with 3 epochs. ptend_q is the bottleneck at -0.07. Analysis: humidity tendencies are notoriously hard - they have heavy-tailed distributions. Need: more epochs, larger model, possibly per-dim normalization. With 23s/epoch, I can do 30-40 epochs in ~15-20 min. Decision: train longer (20 epochs) with current architecture as a strong baseline, then iterate on architecture if needed.

trajectory.description: Observation: 50 epochs of bigger model got R2_eval=0.5794, only slightly better than 20-epoch run (0.5748). Plateau by epoch ~38. Analysis: We're plateauing around 0.58 with current architecture. Need a different approach. The data has vertical structure (60 levels) — using 1D CNN or transformer along the vertical dimension would exploit this. Also ptend_q is the bottleneck. Let me submit eval to lock in best score, then try a vertical CNN/transformer architecture.

trajectory.description: Observation: Plateauing at 0.58 with MLP. ptend_q is bottleneck (0.31). Analysis: Need vertical-structure-aware architecture. Build a model that reshapes inputs as [60 levels, channels] and uses a 1D CNN or transformer along levels. This should better capture the vertical physics (convection, radiation transfer). Decision: implement a 1D conv/transformer encoder over vertical levels, with scalar features broadcast to all levels.

Observation: VerticalTransformer is too slow (347s/epoch) and not yet better. Best is still MLP ckpt at R2=0.517. With 2.79h left, I should abandon the slow transformer and instead push the MLP further. Analysis: MLP plateaued at ~0.58 val. I should try a wider MLP with more regularization, maybe include vertical conv as a hybrid. Better idea: train a larger MLP with strong residual blocks + GeLU + dropout, AND use L1+MSE Huber loss to better handle ptend_q heavy tails. Decision: revert to MLP architecture but bigger, with longer training and Huber/MSE loss. Use ~50 epochs at ~50s/epoch = ~42min.

Observation: Hybrid model failed badly (R2 0.18, with NaN spikes during training). Best checkpoint is still the MLP from iter 9 with R2=0.517. We have only 0.87 hours left and 18 iterations. The hybrid run wasted 1.9h. Best safe option: ensure we keep the best ResMLP checkpoint and submit final eval. Decision: Don't train more — the existing best.pt was overwritten by the failing hybrid run! Need to check this. Actually the hybrid ran and saved its own checkpoint as best.pt overwriting ResMLP. I need to retrain ResMLP quickly to recover, then submit.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best val r2 still 0.5736 from warm-start; training plateaued. We have 0.44h left. Best.pt was overwritten only if new best beat init — it did not (best stayed 0.5736 init), so best.pt should still be the warm-started model. But wait — the script saves only if r2_full > best, and best=0.5736 from init, so best.pt was NOT overwritten. Good. Decision: just submit eval on current best.pt to lock the 0.517 score (which seems to be our actual eval score). Skip more training to save time.
